run_name: NATP Module 16: Death and Dying — Source-First Dry Run
purpose: The Nurse Assistant is introduced to the various stages of the grieving process and physical signs of approaching death. This unit introduces death as a normal stage of life. The health care provider must recognize the physical, psychological, and spiritual needs of the patient/resident during this period to understand coping mechanisms and provide support to the patient/resident and family members. Terminology 14. Hospice 1. Acceptance 15. Living will 2. Advanced directives 16. Morgue 3. Apnea 17. Mottling 4. Anger 18. Palliative care 5. Artificial hydration/nutrition 19. Patient Care Self-Determination Act 6. Autopsy 20. Postmortem care 7. Bargaining 21. Prognosis 8. Cachexia 22. Reminiscence 9. Cheyne-stokes respirations 23. Reincarnation 10. Denial 24. Rigor mortis 11. Depression 25. Shroud 12. Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) 26. Terminal 13. Durable power of attorney

objectives.objective_title: Describe approaches to meet common emotional and spiritual needs of terminally ill patients/residents and their families
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Describe approaches to meet common emotional and spiritual needs of terminally ill patients/residents and their families
objectives.source_excerpt: learning purposes Describe approaches to meet common emotional and spiritual needs of terminally ill patients/residents and their families. A. Lecture/Discussion A. Written test A. Emotional needs of the dying B. Handout 13.2 Timeline B. Class participation 1. Contact with loved ones of Life C. "The Death Exercise" in Instant Teaching Tools for the New Millennium by Michele Deck A. Lecture/Discussion A. Written test B. Role play providing B. Displays supportive support to family of behaviors when caring a. Encourage family members to visit dying patient/resident. Module 16: Death and Dying b. Provide privacy Practice what to say. 2. Communication: This is a difficult skill. for terminally ill a. Listening Students can give patients/residents and b. Touching suggestions for what families 3. Expression of emotions, i.e. guilt, anger, frustration, anxiety, words are most depression comforting/supportive 4. Reminiscence a. Recalling life experiences b. Listening important B. Cultural and spiritual needs of the dying 1. Beliefs are communicated and respected 2. Rites and rituals are encouraged 3. Respect for solitude vs. companionship are explored and respected 4. Beliefs and wishes regarding autopsy, funeral and post- mortem care discussed and respected C. Approaches 1. Respect for religious/cultural practices 2. Provide physical, emotional and spiritual comfort to patient/resident and family 3. Accept patient's/resident's emotions 4. Report to licensed nurse any emotional, social, or spiritual needs

objectives.objective_title: List the rights of the dying patient/resident
objectives.exact_objective_statement: List the rights of the dying patient/resident
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture/Discussion A. Written Test List the rights of the dying patient/resident. B. Handout 13.4 Dying B. Class participation C. Respects and supports A. I have the right to be treated as a living human being until I die Person's Bill of Rights the dying B. I have the right to maintain a sense of hopefulness, however patient's/resident's changing its focus may be rights C. I have the right to be cared for by those who can maintain a sense of hopefulness, however changing this may be D. I have the right to express my feelings and emotions about my approaching death in my own way Module 16: Death and Dying E. I have the right to participate in decisions concerning my care A. Written test B. Class participation F. I have the right to expect continuing medical care and nursing attention even though "cure" goals must be changed to "comfort" goals G. I have the right to not die alone H. I have the right to be free from pain I. I have the right to have my questions answered honestly J. I have the right not be deceived K. I have the right to have help from and for my family in accepting my death L. I have the right to die in peace and dignity M. I have the right to retain my individuality and not be judged for my decisions which may be contrary to beliefs of others N. I have the right to discuss and enlarge my religious and/or spiritual experiences, whatever these may mean to others O. I have the right to expect that the sanctity of the human body will be respected after death P. I have the right to be cared for by caring, sensitive, knowledgeable people who will attempt to und

objectives.objective_title: Differentiate between common signs of approaching death and biological death
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Differentiate between common signs of approaching death and biological death
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture Differentiate between common signs of approaching death and B. Discussion biological death. A. Impending signs of death 1. Decreasing level of consciousness 2. Cold hands and feet 3. Diaphoresis 4. Pale skin 5. Loss of muscle tone 6. Labored or Cheyne-Stokes respirations 7. "Death rattle" Module 16: Death and Dying 8. Weak, irregular, or slow pulse A. Written test 9. Blank, staring expression B. Provides appropriate 10. Jaw drops 11. Mottling of skin care/comfort measure for terminally ill 12. Peristalsis slows patient/resident 13. Loss of sensation 14. Pain decreases B. Signs of biological death 1. No pulse 2. No breathing 3. No blood pressure 4. Pupils maybe fixed and dilated C. Hearing is the last sense to disappear 1. Be professional in all conversation 2. Talk to patient/resident normally 3. Explain procedures
objectives.weight_reason: Deterministic source-density allocation from source minimum theory hours; assessment/clinical/certificate time excluded.

objectives.objective_title: Identify care and comfort measures for dying patients/residents
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Identify care and comfort measures for dying patients/residents
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture Identify care and comfort measures for the dying patient/resident. B. Discussion A. Monitor patient/resident, observe for signs, change of status C. Case studies or 1. Notify licensed nurse in charge for any changes in condition scenarios on terminally 2. Determine patient's/resident's code status (DNR, no ill clients code, etc.) in order to act at appropriate level of care 3. Patient/resident can usually hear, even when they appear unconscious B. Care and comfort measures 1. Pain management 2. Hygiene 3. Oral hygiene 4. Communication/support 5. Positioning/turning 6. Provide comfort 7. Attend to psychosocial needs 8. Spiritual support

objectives.objective_title: Identify the philosophy and goals of hospice and the Nurse Assistant's role when the patient/resident is cared for by a licensed hospice nurse
objectives.exact_objective_statement: Identify the philosophy and goals of hospice and the Nurse Assistant's role when the patient/resident is cared for by a licensed hospice nurse
objectives.source_excerpt: A. Lecture Module 16: Death and Dying Identify the philosophy and goals of hospice and the Nurse B. Discussion A. Written test Assistant's role when the patient/resident is cared for by a B. Class participation licensed hospice nurse. A. History � The first modern hospice, St. Christopher's Hospice began in London in the 1960s B. Philosophy 1. To provide comfort, support and dignity to the dying patient/resident using a holistic approach 2. To provide comfort and support to family and friends of the dying person 3. Hospice care does not prolong life nor does it hasten death 4. Nurse on call to answer questions, provide support, make a visit, or problem solve C. Goals 1. To provide palliative care using a team approach with an emphasis on pain control and comfort, rather than recovery 2. Usually for patients with six months or less to live 3. Patient/resident encouraged to participate in family life and decision- making 4. Services and counseling available to family even after person's death D. Nurse Assistant's role � To provide care within scope of practice as identified by the multidisciplinary team E. Attitudes and skills useful in hospice care 1. Listening 2. Respecting privacy 3. Sensitivity to individual needs 4. Awareness of your own feelings 5. Recognizing stress 6. Care for yourself F. Role of the hospice nurse � To meet the physical, emotional and spiritual needs of terminally ill patient and his/her family Module 16: Death and Dying
